summary: Gruber argues that despite Android's explosive sales growth, the platform lacks compelling exclusive third-party apps. He categorizes Android apps into Google's own, cross-platform ports, and Android exclusives — finding the third category almost entirely composed of system utilities and low-quality software. He introduces the concept of iOS as an 'app console' akin to game consoles, where Apple has built an ecosystem that encourages developers to create high-quality paid apps, while Android functions more like a PC with task killers and home screen replacements. He concludes that Android is succeeding as a phone platform but iOS dominates as a software platform, a split unprecedented in tech industry rivalries.
key_points: Android's exclusive third-party apps are overwhelmingly system utilities (task killers, home screen replacements, file managers) rather than consumer-grade creative or entertainment apps
iOS functions as an 'app console' — a model where Apple, developers, and users all participate in a curated, paid-app ecosystem — while Android resembles a PC or the jailbreak Cydia store
Despite Android surpassing iOS in phone sales, developer support for high-quality exclusive apps has not grown commensurately with handset sales
The popularity of task killers and anti-virus apps on Android actively erodes the consumer trust that a console model requires
Windows Phone 7, only one month old, had already secured big-name game exclusives that Android still lacked
money_quotes: Where are the great, or even good, exclusive third-party apps for Android?
The best games exclusive to Android were written for a mid-'90s console system from Nintendo.
The Android Market, as a whole, bears a lot more resemblance to the Cydia app store than it does to Apple's official App Store.
The mere existence of things like task killers and anti-virus apps for Android — let alone that such utilities are popular — erodes consumer trust.
Inherent to the console model is that third-party software can't — not shouldn't but can't — damage or gum up the system.
Android is a success mostly as a mobile phone — voice and text messaging — and as a client device for Google's services and the mobile web.
The iPhone is clearly in close competition against Android handsets in the mobile phone market. But iOS, as a platform, almost completely dominates the mobile app console market.
iOS's exclusivity for a bunch of big-name mobile games has been broken. Not by Android, where none of these games exist, but by Windows Phone 7, a one-month-old platform.
key_insight: Android's surging phone sales mask a fundamental weakness: it lacks the app console ecosystem that makes iOS a dominant software platform, not just a phone OS.
